what: Memory model — the rule that bites if you forget it
title: CLAUDE.md — Working conventions for AI contributors
text: Every PDFium handle (FPDF_DOCUMENT, FPDF_PAGE, etc.) lives behind R externalptr C finalizer registered via R_RegisterCFinalizerEx(..., TRUE). finalizer path calls FPDF_*Close*. runs, calls R_ClearExternalPtr pointer reads NULL subsequent access. makes pdf_close() safely idempotent. Children (pages, page objects) hold R-level reference parent (doc, page) GC can’t reclaim parent child. Always set parent prot slot child’s externalptr. Automatic close GC works — see vignettes/architecture.Rmd. large documents platform-sensitive code (Windows file-handle blocking deletion), call pdf_close() explicitly.

what: Testing — must be safe under parallel execution
title: CLAUDE.md — Working conventions for AI contributors
text: DESCRIPTION sets Config/testthat/parallel: true. Every test-*.R runs subprocess. Never assume specific test-file order. Never share mutable state across files. Use withr::local_* / withr::defer() cleanup. Helpers go tests/testthat/helper-*.R (sourced every worker). Setup ’s truly -per-worker goes tests/testthat/setup.R. Fixtures load fixture_path("name") (see helper-fixtures.R). Never embed hardcoded path. valgrind ASan runs override Config/testthat/parallel false — don’t write tests require parallelism pass.

what: Memory: what happens when you forget to close
title: How pdfium is built
text: safe. pdfium registers C finalizer every PDF handle. R object becomes unreachable, R’s garbage collector reclaims ; finalizer calls PDFium’s FPDF_CloseDocument release underlying memory. caveat GC eventual, deterministic. open many large documents tight loop, may exhaust process memory GC catches . case, close explicitly: pdf_close() idempotent: calling twice -op. finalizer notices handle already closed skips close call. can safely combine explicit close automatic fallback.
code: inspect <- function(path) {   doc <- pdf_open(path)   pdf_page_count(doc)   # `doc` goes out of scope here.   # R's GC will eventually finalize it and call FPDF_CloseDocument. } inspect("report.pdf") inspect <- function(path) {   doc <- pdf_open(path)   on.exit(pdf_close(doc), add = TRUE)   pdf_page_count(doc) }

title: How pdfium is built
text: call pdf_load_page(doc, ...), returned pdfium_page holds internal reference parent pdfium_doc. Even drop reference doc (explicitly close ), page stays valid page object collected. underlying PDFium document kept alive background last page (object) depends goes away. order : child references parent, never way around.
code: load_page <- function(path) {   doc <- pdf_open(path)   page <- pdf_load_page(doc, 1)   # available in Phase 1+   pdf_close(doc)                  # this is fine   page                            # still usable here }

title: Extracting path geometry
text: lowest-level entry point pdf_path_segments(obj) single "path"-typed pdfium_obj. row one path segment: Columns: segment_index — 1-based segment index within path. segment_type — one "moveto", "lineto", "bezierto", "unknown". x, y — segment coordinates PDF user space (points, origin bottom-left). close_figure — TRUE final segment closed sub-path. note Bezier curves: PDFium stores cubic curve three consecutive "bezierto" rows — two control points followed endpoint. v0.1.0 readout returns three separate rows. companion accessor pdf_path_bezier_controls() returns pair control points alongside endpoint gated upstream PDFium patch (CL 147810); see ADR-009 full rationale status.
code: doc <- pdf_open(fixture) page <- pdf_load_page(doc, 1L) paths <- Filter(function(o) o$type == "path", pdf_page_objects(page)) length(paths) #> [1] 4  pdf_path_segments(paths[[1L]]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   segment_index segment_type     x     y close_figure #>           <int> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>        #> 1             1 moveto           0     0 FALSE        #> 2             2 lineto         288     0 FALSE        #> 3             3 lineto         288   216 FALSE        #> 4             4 lineto           0   216 FALSE        #> 5             5 lineto           0     0 TRUE

title: Extracting path geometry
text: Stroke fill colour come back 4-element numeric vectors (red, green, blue, alpha) values 0..255. NA’s indicate “stroke” “fill” — paths can either, , neither: Dash pattern tibble pattern (-lengths) phase (offset pattern): path’s transformation matrix (CTM applied local coordinates) comes pdf_obj_matrix():
code: pdf_path_stroke(paths[[1L]]) #>   red green  blue alpha width  #>     0     0     0   255     1 pdf_path_fill(paths[[1L]]) #>   red green  blue alpha  #>   255   255   255   255 pdf_path_dash(paths[[1L]]) #> $array #> numeric(0) #>  #> $phase #> [1] 0 pdf_obj_matrix(paths[[1L]]) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    0    0 #> [2,]    0   -1  216 #> [3,]    0    0    1

title: Extracting path geometry
text: consumers like kmextract want one row per segment across page style folded , pdf_extract_paths() batched API: result tibble. Schema: path_index — 1-based path-object index within page (paths ; non-path objects don’t appear). segment_index, segment_type, x, y, close_figure — content pdf_path_segments(). stroke_red/green/blue/alpha, fill_red/green/blue/alpha — stroke fill colours, 0..255. NA paths stroke / fill. stroke_width — stroke width user space; NA unstroked paths. bounds_left/bottom/right/top — path’s bounding box PDF user space. Three attributes carry per-page context: page_size — named numeric vector width height PDF points. page_rotation — integer 0/90/180/270 (page’s /Rotate attribute). text_runs — tibble every text run page, shaped like output pdf_text_runs(). Convenient downstream consumers want position labels relative curve.
code: all_paths <- pdf_extract_paths(fixture) all_paths #> # A tibble: 14 × 19 #>    path_index segment_index segment_type     x     y close_figure stroke_red #>         <int>         <int> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>             <dbl> #>  1          1             1 moveto          0    0   FALSE                 0 #>  2          1             2 lineto        288    0   FALSE                 0 #>  3          1             3 lineto        288  216   FALSE                 0 #>  4          1             4 lineto          0  216   FALSE                 0 #>  5          1             5 lineto          0    0   TRUE                  0 #>  6          2             1 moveto         44   41.3 FALSE               255 #>  7          2             2 lineto        177.  41.3 FALSE               255 #>  8          2             3 lineto        177. 175.  FALSE               255 #>  9          2             4 lineto         44  175.  FALSE               255 #> 10          2             5 lineto         44   41.3 TRUE                255 #> 11          3             1 moveto        144  175.  FALSE                 0 #> 12          3             2 lineto        244   41.3 FALSE                 0 #> 13          4             1 moveto         44   41.3 FALSE                 0 #> 14          4             2 lineto        244  175.  FALSE                 0 #> # ℹ 12 more variables: stroke_green <dbl>, stroke_blue <dbl>, #> #   stroke_alpha <dbl>, stroke_width <dbl>, fill_red <dbl>, fill_green <dbl>, #> #   fill_blue <dbl>, fill_alpha <dbl>, bounds_left <dbl>, bounds_bottom <dbl>, #> #   bounds_right <dbl>, bounds_top <dbl> attr(all_paths, "page_size") #>  width height  #>    288    216 attr(all_paths, "page_rotation") #> [1] 0 nrow(attr(all_paths, "text_runs")) #> [1] 1

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: returned object S3 handle wrapping PDFium FPDF_DOCUMENT. R’s garbage collector calls FPDF_CloseDocument handle becomes unreachable, don’t close explicitly — large documents need delete source file afterward (Windows blocks deletion open files), close pdf_close(): pdf_page_count() pdf_doc_info() also accept path directly want one-shot inspection. package opens closes document case:
code: fixture <- system.file("extdata", "fixtures", "shapes.pdf",                        package = "pdfium") doc <- pdf_open(fixture) doc #> <pdfium_doc [open] /home/runner/work/_temp/Library/pdfium/extdata/fixtures/shapes.pdf> pdf_count <- pdf_page_count(doc) pdf_count #> [1] 1 pdf_page_count(fixture) #> [1] 1

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: pdf_doc_info() returns page count, file version, every standard Info-dictionary entry, POSIXct parses two date fields. shape mirrors pdftools::pdf_info() easy porting: single tag use pdf_doc_meta(doc, "Producer"). standard tags PDFium recognises "Title", "Author", "Subject", "Keywords", "Creator", "Producer", "CreationDate", "ModDate", "Trapped". Missing tags return empty string. date string PDF format ("D:YYYYMMDDHHmmSS+HH'mm'"), pdf_parse_date() parses UTC POSIXct. ’s vectorised accepts truncated forms:
code: info <- pdf_doc_info(doc) info$page_count #> [1] 1 info$file_version       # PDFium reports 10 * major + minor (17 = PDF 1.7) #> [1] 17 info$producer #> [1] "cairo 1.18.0 (https://cairographics.org)" info$creation_date_parsed #> [1] "2026-05-15 19:12:28 UTC" pdf_parse_date(c("D:20240115123045Z", "D:2024")) #> [1] "2024-01-15 12:30:45 UTC" "2024-01-01 00:00:00 UTC"

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: page loaded pdf_load_page(doc, page_num). returned handle carries reference parent doc GC ordering safe: Every drawable element page page object. pdf_page_objects() returns list typed pdfium_obj handles: object’s type one "path" (vector path line/curve segments), "text" (text run), "image" (embedded raster), "form" (Form XObject - reusable sub-page; see section ), "shading" (smooth-shading object). can read object’s bounding box page PDF user space (points, origin page’s bottom-left): transformation matrix (CTM applied object drawn): accessors work every page object pdf_obj_type(), pdf_obj_bounds(), pdf_obj_matrix(). Type-specific accessors follow naming conventions: pdf_path_*, pdf_text_*, pdf_image_*.
code: page <- pdf_load_page(doc, 1L) pdf_page_size(doc, 1L)        # width and height in PDF points #>  width height  #>    288    216 pdf_page_rotation(doc, 1L)    # 0, 90, 180, or 270 degrees #> [1] 0 objs <- pdf_page_objects(page) length(objs) #> [1] 5 vapply(objs, function(o) o$type, character(1)) #> [1] "path" "path" "path" "path" "text" pdf_obj_bounds(objs[[1L]]) #>   left bottom  right    top  #>      0      0    288    216 pdf_obj_matrix(objs[[1L]]) #>      [,1] [,2] [,3] #> [1,]    1    0    0 #> [2,]    0   -1  216 #> [3,]    0    0    1

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: path objects, pdf_path_segments() returns tibble segment coordinates plus close_figure flag segment: Stroke fill colour, dash pattern, miscellaneous style come back separate accessors: one-call extraction returns one row per segment across every path every page — shape kmextract similar consumers expect — use pdf_extract_paths(path_or_doc): result tibble one row per path segment, plus per-path attributes (stroke/fill color, page size, etc.) text_runs attribute carrying page’s text tabular form. See extracting-paths vignette full schema.
code: paths <- Filter(function(o) o$type == "path", objs) pdf_path_segments(paths[[1L]]) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   segment_index segment_type     x     y close_figure #>           <int> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>        #> 1             1 moveto           0     0 FALSE        #> 2             2 lineto         288     0 FALSE        #> 3             3 lineto         288   216 FALSE        #> 4             4 lineto           0   216 FALSE        #> 5             5 lineto           0     0 TRUE pdf_path_stroke(paths[[1L]]) #>   red green  blue alpha width  #>     0     0     0   255     1 pdf_path_fill(paths[[1L]]) #>   red green  blue alpha  #>   255   255   255   255 pdf_path_dash(paths[[1L]]) #> $array #> numeric(0) #>  #> $phase #> [1] 0 extracted <- pdf_extract_paths(fixture) str(extracted, max.level = 1L) #> tibble [14 × 19] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) #>  - attr(*, "page_size")= Named num [1:2] 288 216 #>   ..- attr(*, "names")= chr [1:2] "width" "height" #>  - attr(*, "page_rotation")= int 0 #>  - attr(*, "text_runs")= tibble [1 × 13] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame) nrow(extracted) #> [1] 14 head(extracted, 3L) #> # A tibble: 3 × 19 #>   path_index segment_index segment_type     x     y close_figure stroke_red #>        <int>         <int> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <lgl>             <dbl> #> 1          1             1 moveto           0     0 FALSE                 0 #> 2          1             2 lineto         288     0 FALSE                 0 #> 3          1             3 lineto         288   216 FALSE                 0 #> # ℹ 12 more variables: stroke_green <dbl>, stroke_blue <dbl>, #> #   stroke_alpha <dbl>, stroke_width <dbl>, fill_red <dbl>, fill_green <dbl>, #> #   fill_blue <dbl>, fill_alpha <dbl>, bounds_left <dbl>, bounds_bottom <dbl>, #> #   bounds_right <dbl>, bounds_top <dbl>

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: rasterise page, pdf_render_page(page_or_doc, dpi) returns pdfium_bitmap object inherits base R’s nativeRaster: pdfium_bitmap directly usable graphics::plot(), graphics::rasterImage(), grid::rasterGrob(). Three converters produce common shapes: save helper pdf_render_to_png(file, ...) writes bitmap straight PNG file via png package (Suggests dependency).
code: bmp <- pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 96) bmp #> <pdfium_bitmap 384x288 @ 96 dpi, page 1 of shapes.pdf> dim(bmp)        # height, width #> [1] 288 384 arr <- as.array(bmp)        # 3D [H, W, 4] doubles in 0..1, like png::writePNG ras <- as.raster(bmp)       # base R "raster" class, hex-color matrix mat <- as.matrix(bmp)       # plain character matrix

title: Getting started with pdfium
text: object-type-specific extraction, see: pdf_image_info() / pdf_image_bitmap() / pdf_image_data() embedded raster images. raw-stream-bytes path (pdf_image_data(decoded = FALSE)) lets save original JPEG/JBIG2/JPEG2000 without re-encoding. pdf_form_objects() walk page objects nested inside Form XObject (reusable sub-page used tiling patterns, glyph procedures, annotation appearance streams, etc.). pdf_obj_clip_path() / pdf_clip_path_segments() read clip geometry attached page object.

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
text: plot() call dispatches plot.pdfium_bitmap(), opens fresh plot window asp = 1 zero margins draws bitmap [graphics::rasterImage()]. Pass interpolate = FALSE want pixel-exact (nearest-neighbour) display small bitmap — useful embedded raster fixtures. bitmap’s dimensions scale linearly dpi. default dpi = 72, one pixel per PDF point: 4 × 3 inch page becomes 288 × 216 pixels. dpi = 144, page 576 × 432.
code: doc <- pdf_open(fixture) bmp <- pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 96) bmp                                   # a one-line summary #> <pdfium_bitmap 384x288 @ 96 dpi, page 1 of shapes.pdf> dim(bmp)                              # height, width in pixels #> [1] 288 384  plot(bmp)                             # uses plot.pdfium_bitmap() dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi =  72)) #> [1] 216 288 dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 144)) #> [1] 432 576

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
text: default background white. Pass colour string grDevices::col2rgb() understands, NA transparent:
code: bmp_red   <- pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, background = "red") bmp_trans <- pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, background = NA)  bmp_red[1L,  1L]                      # top-left pixel #> [1] -1 bmp_trans[1L, 1L]                     # depends on whether page content covers #> [1] -1

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
text: Rotation degrees, applied top page’s /Rotate attribute. Rotating 90 270 swaps bitmap’s width height:
code: dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, rotation =   0)) #> [1] 216 288 dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, rotation =  90)) #> [1] 288 216 dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, rotation = 180)) #> [1] 216 288 dim(pdf_render_page(doc, dpi = 72, rotation = 270)) #> [1] 288 216

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
code: # A 3D numeric array [H, W, 4] with values in 0..1 - matches the # format png::writePNG() and pdftools::pdf_render_page() both produce. arr <- as.array(bmp) dim(arr) #> [1] 288 384   4 range(arr) #> [1] 0 1  # Base R "raster" object - character matrix of "#RRGGBBAA" hex colors. ras <- as.raster(bmp) ras[1L, 1L] #>      [,1]        #> [1,] "#FFFFFFFF"  # A plain character matrix (drops the "raster" class). mat <- as.matrix(bmp) class(mat) #> [1] "matrix" "array"

code: out <- tempfile(fileext = ".png") pdf_render_to_png(doc, file = out, dpi = 96) file.exists(out) #> [1] TRUE file.size(out) #> [1] 5679

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
text: pdf_render_page(annotations = TRUE) paints annotation appearance streams top page. shapes.pdf annotations flag -op ; annotated PDFs rendered bitmap visibly changes.

title: Rendering pages to bitmaps
text: "image"-typed page object, three accessors return rendered raw image data pdfium_bitmap shape: saving original embedded asset without re-encoding, raw-bytes path useful: filters contains "DCTDecode", raw bytes original JPEG; contains "JPXDecode", ’re JPEG 2000; "FlateDecode" ’re Deflate-compressed pixels. See pdf_image_filters() full enumeration common decoders.
code: img_fixture <- system.file("extdata", "fixtures", "image.pdf",                            package = "pdfium") img_doc <- pdf_open(img_fixture) img_page <- pdf_load_page(img_doc, 1L) imgs <- Filter(function(o) o$type == "image", pdf_page_objects(img_page))  # Decoded source-pixel bitmap (no CTM applied). src <- pdf_image_bitmap(imgs[[1L]]) dim(src) #> [1] 16 16  # CTM-applied rendering (what a viewer would actually draw). viewer <- pdf_image_rendered(imgs[[1L]]) dim(viewer) #> [1] 134 134 filters <- pdf_image_filters(imgs[[1L]]) filters #> [1] "FlateDecode" raw_bytes <- pdf_image_data(imgs[[1L]], decoded = FALSE) length(raw_bytes) #> [1] 32

title: Extracting text and fonts
text: pdf_text_runs(page) returns one row per text-typed page object page, content + position + font single tibble: Columns (use names(runs) canonical list — names match schema PDFium reports today): object_index — 1-based page-object index. Matches index pdf_page_objects(page). bbox_left, bbox_bottom, bbox_right, bbox_top — bounding box PDF user space (points, origin bottom-left). font_size_pt — font size PDFium reports (user-space points, text matrix applied). text — Unicode content run, UTF-8 encoded. font_base_name — BaseFont font dictionary. font_family — FontFamily present. font_weight — integer 100..900 (CSS-style; 400 ≈ regular, 700 ≈ bold). font_italic_angle — italic angle degrees, 0 upright fonts. font_is_embedded — logical; TRUE font program embedded. font_flags — PDF font-descriptor /Flags bitmask (fixed-pitch, serif, symbolic, …).
code: runs <- pdf_text_runs(page) runs #> # A tibble: 5 × 13 #>   text_index bounds_left bounds_bottom bounds_right bounds_top font_size text  #>        <int>       <dbl>         <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>     <dbl> <chr> #> 1          2        130.          171.         158.       180.         1 Hello #> 2          3        129.          137.         158.       146.         1 world #> 3          4        126.          103.         138.       114.         1 pd    #> 4          5        140.          105.         145.       114.         1 fi    #> 5          6        146.          105.         162.       112.         1 um    #> # ℹ 6 more variables: font_base_name <chr>, font_family <chr>, #> #   font_weight <int>, font_italic_angle <int>, font_is_embedded <lgl>, #> #   font_flags <int>

"widget" annotations AcroForm fields; pass document pdf_form_fields() field-level metadata. flags integer - annotation's 32-bit flag bitmask. title: Permission flags from a PDF's encryption dictionary — pdf_doc_permissions
text: Wraps FPDF_GetDocPermissions. decoded flags follow PDF specification's /P (UserAccess) bit assignments (ISO 32000-1 section 7.6.3.2, Table 22): print - bit 3: print document. modify - bit 4: change content annotation / form-field values. copy - bit 5: copy otherwise extract text graphics document. annotate - bit 6: add modify text annotations. fill_forms - bit 9: fill interactive form fields, regardless modify. extract_for_a11y - bit 10: extract text graphics accessibility purposes. assemble - bit 11: insert, rotate, delete pages create bookmarks / thumbnails, regardless modify. print_high_res - bit 12: faithful digital print copy. FALSE print TRUE, document may print low resolution.

Encoded UTF-16LE handed PDFium; character representable UTF-8 works (including supplementary-plane code points via surrogate pairs). case_sensitive TRUE, exact-case matches returned. Default FALSE (case-insensitive ASCII letters; PDFium promise case folding non-ASCII letters). whole_word TRUE, match must bounded word-break characters (whitespace / punctuation) sides. title: Plot a pdfium_bitmap — plot.pdfium_bitmap
text: go .array(x) rather handing integer matrix directly graphics::rasterImage() two reasons compound: Per documented raster contract (see ?grDevices::.raster, "Raster images internally represented row-first"), "raster" nativeRaster objects must row-major memory layout. R's .raster.matrix() transposes input precisely satisfy . integer matrix comes C++ standard R column-major matrix, feeding directly non-conformant shows diagonal stripe artifacts detailed content. rasterImage plot.window uses user-coordinate system, defaults (xaxs = "r", yaxs = "r") padding interval 4% side — silently compressing raster ~92% device forcing sub-pixel resampling. grid::grid.raster() uses npc coordinates subject . Going .array(x) 3-D c(H, W, 4) numeric array rendering grid::grid.raster() sidesteps : array path uses positional channel storage (row-vs-column convention), grid coordinates 0..1 npc without padding.
